learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Sorting, Intervals, Merge Intervals, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine scheduling a company-wide meeting. Every employee already has meetings on their calendar. To find a suitable meeting slot, combine everyone's schedules into one timeline and identify the gaps where nobody is busy.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine scheduling a company-wide meeting. Every employee already has meetings on their calendar. To find a suitable meeting slot, combine everyone's schedules into one timeline and identify the gaps where nobody is busy.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this Merge Intervals pattern whenever multiple people's schedules must be combined to find overlapping busy or free periods.
learning.signals: Whenever multiple schedules or intervals must be combined to find available gaps, think of Merge Intervals.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: We are given a list schedule of employees, where schedule[i] represents the working intervals of the ith employee. Each employee has a list of non-overlapping intervals sorted by start time. Return the list of finite intervals representing the common free time for all employees.

examples.explanation: Every employee is free only during the interval [3,4].
The common free intervals are [5,6] and [7,9].

constraints: Intervals within each employee are already sorted and non-overlapping.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Compare the working intervals of every employee with every other employee to find common free periods.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Instead of comparing every employee with every other employee, merge all working intervals into one sorted timeline. Any gap between merged intervals represents a common free time shared by everyone.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: Once all busy intervals are merged, every gap between them is a common free interval.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever multiple schedules or intervals must be combined to find available gaps, think of Merge Intervals.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Sorting once allows all overlapping working intervals to be merged in a single traversal. Every gap between merged busy intervals is guaranteed to be a common free interval.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever multiple schedules or intervals must be combined to find available gaps, think of Merge Intervals.

pattern.name: Merge Intervals
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever multiple schedules or intervals must be combined to find available gaps, think of Merge Intervals.

approaches.title: Compare Every Schedule
approaches.idea: Compare the working intervals of every employee with every other employee to find common free periods.
approaches.dryRun:
Compare employee 1 with employee 2, then employee 3.
Very inefficient for many employees.

code.java: Brute force comparison omitted because of high complexity.

approaches.title: Flatten and Sort
approaches.idea: Collect every interval into one list, sort them by start time, and merge overlapping intervals.

approaches.title: Merge Intervals
approaches.idea: Flatten all employee schedules into a single list, sort by starting time, merge overlapping intervals, and record every gap between consecutive merged intervals as common free time.
approaches.intuition: Sorting once allows all overlapping working intervals to be merged in a single traversal. Every gap between merged busy intervals is guaranteed to be a common free interval.
algorithm: Flatten all employee intervals into one list.
Whenever a gap exists between merged intervals, record it.

code.cpp: Uses sorting followed by interval merging to collect free gaps.
code.javascript: Uses interval sorting and merge algorithm.

visualization.description: Merge all busy intervals and the remaining gaps become common free time.

interviewFollowUps: Can this be solved using a Priority Queue?
How would you handle millions of employees?
Can you find the earliest available meeting slot?
How would you return free intervals of a minimum duration?
Can this be solved using a Sweep Line algorithm?
interviewTips: Mention that each employee's schedule is already sorted.
Explain why flattening simplifies the problem.
Describe interval merging before finding gaps.
State O(N log N) complexity clearly.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: Once all busy intervals are merged, every gap between them is a common free interval.

importantPoints: Intervals are already sorted per employee.
